exercises.title: None vs Other Falsy Values
exercises.description: None, 0, '', and False are all falsy — but they are different. Use 'is None' and 'is not None' to test for None, and print the comparison results.
exercises.starterCode:
values = [None, 0, "", False, 0.0]

# TODO: For each value, print whether it IS None
for v in values:
    print(v is None)
hints: Use 'is None' (not == None) to check for None.
Only None itself passes the 'is None' test.

exercises.title: Functions That Return None
exercises.description: A function with no return statement implicitly returns None. Demonstrate this by calling print() itself and capturing its return value.
exercises.starterCode:
# print() returns None — it produces output as a side effect
result = print("This is the side effect")

# TODO: Print what 'result' contains
print(result)

# TODO: Print the type of result
print(type(result))

# TODO: Check if result is None
print(result is None)
exercises.expectedOutput:
This is the side effect
None
<class 'NoneType'>
True
hints: print() returns None — calling print() produces output but doesn't compute a value.

exercises.title: Using None as a Sentinel Value
exercises.description: None is commonly used to represent 'not yet set'. Use 'is not None' to check before using a value, and print different messages depending on whether data is available.
exercises.starterCode:
# Simulating optional data
user_email = "alice@example.com"
user_phone = None   # user didn't provide a phone number

# TODO: Check if user_email is not None before printing it
if user_email is not None:
    print(f"Email: {user_email}")
else:
    print("Email: not provided")

# TODO: Check if user_phone is not None before printing it
if user_phone is not None:
    print(f"Phone: {user_phone}")
else:
    print("Phone: not provided")

# TODO: Print the type of user_phone
print(type(user_phone))
hints: Use 'is not None' to check if a value was actually set.
This pattern is common when data is optional.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: None is Unique
warmup.description: None represents 'no value'. It is falsy but different from 0, '', or False.
warmup.starterCode:
x = None
print(x)
print(type(x))
print(x is None)   # correct way to check
print(bool(x))     # None is falsy
